Currency check (critical for a fast-moving-field claim ~16 months old as of today): METR published a direct follow-up, "Time Horizon 1.1" (metr.org/blog/2026-1-29-time-horizon-1-1), which re-derives the trend with an updated methodology. Result: the full 2019-2025 period *still* comes out to "exactly the same... 196 days (7 months)," reaffirming rather than overturning the original headline number. The update does show the *recent* sub-trend is faster (since 2023: ~131 days/4.3 months; since 2024: ~89 days/3 months) — but this is consistent with, not contradictory to, the original claim's own hedge that "the trend may have accelerated in 2024."

Net assessment: the claim accurately represents its cited primary source, the source is a specialized, widely-cited empirical AI-evaluation study (not a marketing claim), and it has been independently reaffirmed by the same team's ~10-month-later replication rather than superseded or debunked. A real methodological critique exists and should be surfaced as a caveat in the taxonomy write-up (data sparsity, task-suite design, single-lab concentration, and the wide 1-4x/year uncertainty band), but it does not rise to overturning the figure — it argues for citing it with appropriate error bars, not for discarding it.

confirmed.claim: As of the paper's measurement, Claude 3.7 Sonnet has a 50% time horizon of around 50 minutes on the tested task set (a mix of RE-Bench, HCAST, and 66 newly created shorter tasks).
confirmed.quote: Claude 3.7 Sonnet have a 50% time horizon of around 50 minutes
confirmed.evidence: Two independent WebFetch reads of the arxiv abstract (arxiv.org/abs/2503.14499 and the v1 HTML version) both returned the identical sentence: "On these tasks, current frontier AI models such as Claude 3.7 Sonnet have a 50% time horizon of around 50 minutes." This is a verbatim match to the claim and its supporting quote. The paper's task set (RE-Bench, HCAST, and 66 newly created shorter/SWAA tasks) matches the claim's description. METR's companion blog post rounds this to "approximately one hour," consistent with ~50 minutes, not contradicting it. A separate METR publication (metr.org/evaluations/claude-3-7-report) reports "around 55 minutes" for Claude 3.7 Sonnet, but that figure is from a different, distinct evaluation ("General Autonomous Capabilities" tasks), not the same task mixture the claim specifies — so it doesn't contradict this claim, it's just a different measurement on a different benchmark. The claim is correctly scoped ("as of the paper's measurement," specific task set named), sourced to the primary paper, and not an overreach.

refuted.claim: METR's time-horizon growth trend across frontier AI models (2019-2025) fits an exponential curve significantly better than linear or hyperbolic alternatives, meaning reliably-completable task duration grows multiplicatively over time rather than additively.

Frontier model task-completion capability (measured by the human-time-equivalent of tasks completed with 50% success) has grown exponentially over 6 years, doubling roughly every 7 months, with Claude 3.7 Sonnet reaching an approximately 1-hour time horizon as of the report.

State-of-the-art LLM agents (e.g., GPT-4o) succeed on fewer than half of τ-bench tasks in single-trial evaluation, quantifying current frontier model unreliability on realistic multi-turn tool-use tasks.

Capability rankings on short/atomic tasks do not predict long-horizon reliability: a 12B model (Mistral Nemo) outperformed a 400B MoE model (Llama 4 Maverick) on long+very-long horizon reliability despite being 33x smaller and ranking behind it on short tasks — a capability-reliability rank inversion invisible to pass@1-only evaluation.

Across all 68 confirmed real-world failures, the single unifying root cause was an unbounded feedback path (no effective stopping condition), with tool-controlled retry loops (41.2%) and model-controlled termination (38.2%) as the two dominant proximate causes -- i.e. agents fail not from missing loop syntax but from loop-exit conditions that silently depend on model output.

System Design Issues is the single largest failure category (44.2% / 41.8% depending on dataset cut), driven heavily by two detectable signals: Step Repetition (FM-1.3, 15.7%) and Unaware of Termination Conditions (FM-1.5, 12.4%) -- both are observable in raw execution telemetry (repeated tool/action calls, absence of a stop signal) without needing semantic judgment.

Failure rates on current SOTA multi-agent frameworks are extremely high — up to 86.7% on some benchmarks — establishing a strong empirical baseline for how unreliable agentic systems are at design time.

Failure onset typically occurs very early in agent trajectories (within the first few execution steps), but remains undetected/silent until recovery is no longer possible — supporting the compounding-error/silent-failure pattern.

In AppWorld, among coding-agent trajectories that make an explicit self-assessed status claim, 75.8% of those claims constitute false success (agent asserts success but the environment state shows failure).

Lightweight TF-IDF-based detectors substantially outperform LLM-judge monitors for catching false success: AUROC 0.83 (tau2-bench, task-disjoint split) and 0.95 (AppWorld), while running about 3,300x faster and catching 4-8x more false successes at matched flag rates — supporting cheap, pre-deployment-style probing over expensive LLM-as-judge evaluation.

A 1% per-step error rate -- which looks acceptable on standard short benchmarks -- is fatal for long-horizon agentic tasks: the paper states a system with such an error rate is 'a system with a 1% per-step error rate is not acceptable' and elsewhere notes it 'results in inevitable failure' at scale, i.e. it is expected to fail after only ~100 steps of a million-step task.

Model reliability decays steeply as task length increases: near-100% success on short tasks but under 10% success on tasks that would take a human expert more than ~4 hours, quantifying the compounding-error / long-horizon degradation phenomenon.

Latitude's framework identifies six agent-specific failure modes distinct from LLM failures: tool misuse, context loss, goal drift, retry loops, cascading errors in multi-agent systems, and silent quality degradation.
